records.evidence: implementation landed: src/worker.py:3 `MAX_ATTEMPTS = 5` and src/worker.py:4 `TIMEOUT_S = 30` match the Decision, and src/worker.py:15-20 `handle_failure` matches the Sketch verbatim (increment, drop at MAX_ATTEMPTS, else push_tail); Problem and Sketch sections are superseded scaffolding. Insight walk, section by section: Options §A (dead-letter queue rejected — unattended deploys, unmonitored DLQ grows without bound), §B (exponential backoff rejected — tail re-entry already spaces attempts, explicit backoff would double-delay), §C (per-job-type timeouts rejected — per-type budgets become tuning decisions nobody owns) each carry rationale for a deliberate absence a future maintainer could wrongly 'fix' → 3 insights; Decision section's checkable bounds (5 attempts, 30s) are already carried by README.md:9-10 and CLAUDE.md:3-4, so no new claims to extract

insights.insight: The absence of a dead-letter queue is deliberate: exhausted jobs are dropped, not parked, because this system deploys unattended — an unmonitored DLQ grows without bound and hides failures better than dropping does. Revisit only if an operator rotation ever exists for this service.

The absence of an explicit backoff schedule between attempts is deliberate: re-entering at the tail already spaces retries by roughly the queue's drain time, so an explicit backoff would double-delay.

The single global `TIMEOUT_S` for all job types is deliberate: per-job-type budgets were rejected because they turn each new job type into a tuning decision nobody owns; fast types wasting budget under the global cap is an accepted cost.

payload.decision_entry:
## 2026-02-14 — worker queue failure handling
- Decided: bounded retries (`MAX_ATTEMPTS = 5`), one global per-attempt timeout (`TIMEOUT_S = 30`), failed jobs re-enter at the tail, drop outright on exhaustion. Rejected: dead-letter queue (unattended deploys — an unmonitored DLQ hides failures), exponential backoff (tail re-entry already spaces attempts; explicit backoff double-delays), per-job-type timeouts (per-type budgets are tuning decisions nobody owns).
- Still binds: revisit a DLQ only if an operator rotation ever exists for this service.
- Code: src/worker.py (MAX_ATTEMPTS, TIMEOUT_S, run_attempt, handle_failure).
- Source: docs/plans/2026-02-14-worker-queue-design.md (retired in this distillation).

records.evidence: implementation landed: src/worker.py:15-20 `handle_failure(queue, job)` matches Task 2's code block verbatim, and src/worker.py:16 `job.attempts += 1` presumes Task 1's `attempts` field; Task 3 is a one-off verification step with nothing durable. Insight walk: Task 1 (add the field), Task 2 (wire the failure path), Task 3 (verify) — insight sweep: none — pure implementation recipe. One claim extracted: the `attempts`-counter mechanism, which no living doc currently names (README.md:9-10 and CLAUDE.md:3-4 carry the bounds, not the field)

claims.claim: Each job carries an `attempts` counter (int, starting at 0), incremented once per failure; `handle_failure` drops the job when it reaches `MAX_ATTEMPTS`, otherwise re-enqueues it at the tail (`src/worker.py:15-20`).
claims.evidence: src/worker.py:16 `job.attempts += 1`; src/worker.py:17-20 `if job.attempts >= MAX_ATTEMPTS: queue.drop(job) else: queue.push_tail(job)`

payload.decision_entry:
## 2026-03-01 — attempts counter on jobs
- Decided: retry bookkeeping lives on the job record as an `attempts` int (default 0); the worker's single failure path `handle_failure` increments it and branches drop-vs-tail-re-entry.
- Code: src/worker.py:15-20 (handle_failure).
- Source: docs/plans/2026-03-01-attempts-field-plan.md (retired in this distillation).
